stories.headline: Democratic socialist Melat Kiros ousts 15-term incumbent Diana DeGette in Colorado's 1st Congressional District primary
stories.summary:
In one of the most stunning upsets in recent Colorado political history, 29-year-old Melat Kiros defeated U.S. Rep. Diana DeGette in Tuesday's Democratic primary for Colorado's 1st Congressional District, which covers the city of Denver. DeGette had held the seat for 29 years and 15 terms, making her one of the longest-serving members of Colorado's congressional delegation. The Associated Press called the race for Kiros, who ran on a democratic socialist platform and sharply criticized Democratic support for Israel's military campaign in Gaza.

The race attracted more than $3 million in combined campaign and independent spending and drew national attention following a wave of progressive primary victories in New York. A poll from the Colorado Polling Institute found that 52 percent of Denver residents hold a positive view of socialism. Kiros is expected to win the general election in November in the heavily Democratic district and take her seat in Congress in January.

The victory adds to a growing progressive insurgency within the Democratic Party that has unsettled party leadership at the state and national level. Colorado Democratic Party chairman Chris Hansen acknowledged voters were signaling a desire for a new direction. DeGette had not faced a serious primary challenge in decades.

stories.headline: Gov. Polis fires two clemency board members who publicly criticized his decision to free Tina Peters
stories.summary:
Colorado Gov. Jared Polis on Wednesday removed two members of his Executive Clemency Advisory Board after they publicly spoke out against his decision to commute the sentence of Tina Peters, the former Mesa County clerk convicted of election-related crimes. Polis fired attorneys Hannah Seigel Proff and Azra Taslimi, citing a breach of the board's required confidentiality, according to letters reported first by the New York Times. The governor's office confirmed the removals to The Denver Post and appointed two new members to fill the vacated seats.

Peters was convicted of four felonies and sentenced in October 2024 to nine years in prison after she gave an associate of election-denier Mike Lindell unauthorized access to Mesa County's voting system following the 2020 election. Polis commuted her sentence in May, arguing it was disproportionately harsh. Peters was released June 1 and has since appeared on conservative media and met with President Donald Trump in the Oval Office on Tuesday.

Seigel Proff and Taslimi had spoken to media and co-authored an opinion piece in The Denver Post in mid-June revealing that the advisory board unanimously recommended denial of clemency for Peters — twice. They argued the governor's action revealed a system that bends for some applicants while holding firm against others. Polis previously faced a formal censure from the state Democratic Party over the Peters commutation.

stories.headline: Progressives sweep metro Denver statehouse primaries, toppling incumbents and overcoming millions in dark-money opposition
stories.summary:
Progressive candidates backed by Colorado's largest labor unions won several Denver-area statehouse Democratic primary races Tuesday, defeating two sitting lawmakers and overcoming more than $2 million in opposition spending from a network of largely opaque PACs. The wins amounted to a clean sweep for the labor-funded outside spending group and reversed, in part, gains made by centrist organization One Main Street in the 2024 cycle.

In Denver's House District 6, progressive challenger Iris Halpern defeated incumbent Rep. Sean Camacho, who conceded Wednesday morning after trailing by roughly 1,300 votes. The district follows the East Colfax Avenue corridor from the state Capitol to the Aurora border. Camacho had himself unseated a left-wing incumbent in 2024 and served as co-chair of the business-friendly Opportunity Caucus, making his loss a significant blow to moderate Democrats in the legislature. More than $1 million in outside money flowed into that race alone.

In Senate District 34, covering parts of downtown and west and northwest Denver, Chela Garcia Irlando crushed Andrés Carrera by nearly 10,000 votes despite more than $1.5 million in combined outside spending. Irlando was endorsed by U.S. Sen. Bernie Sanders and labor icon Dolores Huerta. In Broomfield, Rep. Kenny Nguyen also held off a One Main Street-backed challenger, completing the labor coalition's sweep of the night's competitive statehouse contests.

stories.headline: Republican gubernatorial primary heads toward recount as Kirkmeyer, Marx separated by hundreds of votes
stories.summary:
The Republican primary race for Colorado governor remained unresolved Wednesday, with state Sen. Barb Kirkmeyer and ministry leader Victor Marx separated by fewer than a few hundred votes and the race entering mandatory recount territory. As of Wednesday afternoon, Marx held 39.9 percent of the vote to Kirkmeyer's 39.5 percent, with tens of thousands of ballots still being counted across multiple counties including roughly 30,000 each in Denver, Boulder and El Paso counties.

A third candidate, state Rep. Scott Bottoms of Colorado Springs, received about 20 percent of the vote. Both Bottoms and Marx competed for the MAGA religious conservative lane within the party, and there was grumbling among some Republican supporters that Bottoms drew votes away from Marx. The eventual Republican nominee will face Democratic Attorney General Phil Weiser in the November general election.

The extended count means Colorado may not know its Republican gubernatorial nominee for several more days. Kirkmeyer and Marx represent different wings of the state GOP, and the outcome will shape the party's general election strategy heading into what is expected to be a competitive race against Weiser.

stories.headline: Nuggets contact LeBron James as NBA free agency opens, sources say
stories.summary:
The Denver Nuggets have reached out to LeBron James to express interest in signing the 41-year-old forward in NBA free agency, a league source told The Denver Post. James is departing the Los Angeles Lakers after eight seasons, a move reported by ESPN's Shams Charania on Tuesday as the league's free agency moratorium began. Cleveland, Golden State, Miami and Minnesota are also among the teams reportedly pursuing James.

The Nuggets have history with James on multiple fronts. Team president Josh Kroenke mailed James a throwback Nuggets jersey in 2018 before he signed with the Lakers, and Kroenke is described as a close personal friend. Nuggets center Nikola Jokic and James have expressed deep mutual admiration, and the two have faced each other in three playoff series this decade, including twice in the Western Conference Finals. Nuggets lead assistant coach Jared Dudley is a former teammate and noted confidant of James.

Despite the compelling connections, Denver faces stiff competition. James's hometown Cavaliers just completed an Eastern Conference Finals run, the Heat recently added Giannis Antetokounmpo, and Golden State can offer him a pairing with longtime friend Steph Curry. According to Charania, James's decision will be driven by personal happiness rather than money. The Nuggets' championship pedigree and the prospect of pairing James with Jokic — widely considered two of the smartest players in basketball history — form the core of Denver's pitch.

stories.headline: Avalanche sign Jaden Schwartz, Noah Juulsen as Joe Sakic wraps active first day as GM
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche opened NHL free agency Wednesday by signing veteran forward Jaden Schwartz to a three-year, $9.75 million contract and defenseman Noah Juulsen to a two-year, $2.2 million deal, giving general manager Joe Sakic a productive first day back in that role. Schwartz, 34, is a Colorado College alumnus who had 11 goals and 26 points in 50 games for the Seattle Kraken last season. He brings 233 career goals and Stanley Cup championship experience from the Blues' 2019 title run.

Sakic described Schwartz as capable of playing anywhere in the top nine and fitting the Avalanche's identity in terms of character and championship pedigree. Juulsen, 29, provides right-handed depth on the blue line after posting a career-high 10 points in 52 games with the Philadelphia Flyers last season. He is projected to be a seventh defenseman option who adds size, physicality and penalty kill ability.

The signings come after Colorado made a trio of trades and nine draft selections at the 2026 NHL Draft. After completing Wednesday's moves, the Avalanche carry slightly under $1.3 million in salary cap space, according to PuckPedia. Sakic said the team is comfortable with its current roster construction while leaving flexibility to make additions at the trade deadline if needed.

stories.headline: Aldi files plans for first Colorado stores in ambitious expansion push
stories.summary:
Discount grocery chain Aldi has filed plans for its first Colorado store locations as part of an expansion the company announced in January that calls for adding 50 outlets across the state. The filings were reported Wednesday by the Denver Business Journal, marking a significant step toward the German-owned chain establishing a retail presence in the Colorado market. Aldi has been rapidly expanding its U.S. footprint in recent years, competing on price with established regional and national grocery chains.

Colorado residents have long had limited access to Aldi locations compared to shoppers in many other states where the chain has operated for decades. The planned expansion would bring the grocer's no-frills, low-cost model to a market currently dominated by King Soopers, Safeway, Sprouts and Walmart. Specific store locations and opening timelines were not immediately available.

stories.headline: Denver restaurant scene sees 26 openings against 12 closings in June, though several longtime staples prepare to shut doors
stories.summary:
Denver's restaurant industry recorded 26 openings against 12 closings in June, according to Westword's monthly tracking, though the closures included several longtime neighborhood staples. RiNo breakfast spot Port Side announced it will close July 5 after a decade of operation, and Table 6 has set a July 9 closure date after 22 years. Rock Bottom Brewing and Church & Union shuttered without advance warning during the month.

Among the notable new arrivals were El Tule in the Denver Tech Center, a high-end Mexican restaurant, and Mother Other on South Broadway, a vegetarian concept. Highland butcher Western Daughters expanded into a wine bar, and Ciao Babe launched an Italian street food stall at Avanti Food & Beverage. Food halls saw activity as well, with Denver Central Market replacing its Curio bar with Bar 77 and new vendors joining Edgewater Public Market and Junction Food & Drink.

The month's activity reflects an ongoing tension in the Denver dining market between closures at established independent restaurants and a continued appetite for new concepts. Westword noted that new openings continue to outpace closings even as some high-profile departures cast a shadow over the scene.

stories.headline: Update: Aspen Acres fire nearly doubles to 48,000 acres in Pueblo and Custer counties, destroying more than 180 structures
stories.summary:
The Aspen Acres fire burning in Pueblo and Custer counties surged to nearly 48,000 acres Wednesday with zero containment, nearly doubling in size from figures reported Tuesday and surpassing the Snyder fire to become Colorado's largest active wildfire. New mandatory evacuation orders were issued Wednesday afternoon as the blaze continued moving fast. Incident command was transferred to the Colorado Division of Fire Prevention and Control, designating it a Type 3 incident managed at the state level.

Pueblo County Sheriff David Lucero confirmed at a Wednesday afternoon press conference that approximately 125 structures have been destroyed in Pueblo County, with an additional 55 structures lost in Custer County, most of them residential homes. The losses include the Horseshoe Lodge and Retreat Center in Beulah. Across all active Colorado wildfires, officials estimate more than 100,000 acres have burned in recent days.

Gov. Jared Polis and state health officials issued guidance Wednesday urging Coloradans to monitor air quality and limit outdoor activity as wildfire smoke spread across the state, including the Denver metro area. The National Weather Service forecast hazy sunshine and high temperatures in the upper 80s to low 90s along the urban corridor through midweek. Red flag fire conditions including low humidity and high winds remained a concern.

stories.headline: Hiker dies on Little Bear Peak after rockfall on notoriously dangerous Sangre de Cristo route
stories.summary:
A hiker died last week after a large section of rock broke loose on Little Bear Peak, a 14,041-foot summit in the Sangre de Cristo Mountains of Alamosa County, causing them to fall a significant distance. The incident occurred June 27 on the West Ridge Indirect approach, an increasingly popular alternative to the peak's standard but rockfall-prone Hourglass route. Rescuers from Alamosa Volunteer Search and Rescue responded in sustained 60 mph winds that initially complicated helicopter evacuation efforts.

Two rescuers were flown by helicopter into Tobin Basin at about 12,100 feet and climbed toward the injured hiker, while a Colorado National Guard Black Hawk helicopter later transported the victim to a search-and-rescue helipad on Lake Como Road. Flight nurses and medics began life-saving efforts on the ground, but the hiker died of their injuries. The victim's name and age had not been publicly released as of Wednesday.

Little Bear Peak is widely regarded as one of Colorado's most technically dangerous fourteeners due to chronic rockfall hazard, particularly in its Hourglass section. Last July, a separate climber was critically injured there after being struck by a boulder and falling roughly 30 feet. The latest fatality comes weeks after a solo trail runner from Montrose also died on Mount Sneffels in the San Juan Mountains.

stories.headline: Communities across Colorado cancel Fourth of July fireworks shows amid ongoing wildfire threat
stories.summary:
Dozens of communities across Colorado have canceled their planned Fourth of July fireworks displays due to active wildfires and fire restrictions in place across much of the state. The cancellations affect celebrations that would otherwise draw large crowds during the Independence Day weekend. Fort Carson, which is hosting its Front Range Freedom Fest on Friday with country artist Tyler Hubbard headlining, also announced it will not hold a fireworks show this year because of high fire risk.

The wave of cancellations comes as more than 100,000 acres have burned across Colorado in recent days, with several major fires still uncontained. Red flag fire conditions — combining low humidity, dry fuels and high winds — have persisted across much of the state and are expected to continue into the holiday weekend. State officials have urged residents to adhere to fire restrictions, which in many jurisdictions prohibit all outdoor burning including consumer fireworks.

Fort Carson's Freedom Fest, running from 4 p.m. to 10 p.m. Friday at Iron Horse Park, will mark the 250th anniversary of the United States and the 150th anniversary of Colorado. The event, which also features X Ambassadors and Bryce Vine, is free and open to the public, with those lacking base access encouraged to apply for a gate pass in advance.

stories.headline: Colorado mountain towns face historic water shortages; state issues emergency orders to prevent communities from running dry
stories.summary:
Historic drought conditions are threatening drinking water supplies in Colorado's mountain towns, prompting state officials to issue emergency orders earlier this month allowing water to be redistributed to communities most at risk of running out. The Colorado River District, representing 15 Western Slope counties, is leading the response effort with nearly $1 million in financial support from the Colorado Water Conservation Board to ensure even towns that cannot afford emergency measures retain access to drinking water.

At the center of the crisis is the Green Mountain Reservoir's historic users pool, a backup water supply that is not projected to fill this year for the first time since the 1960s. State Engineer Jason Ullmann authorized emergency releases allowing the district to use its water supplies more flexibly through the end of August. Large agricultural water users in the Grand Valley also agreed to voluntarily reduce their consumption to ease pressure on the Colorado River and protect small towns and ranchers with junior water rights.

The Eagle River Water and Sanitation District, which serves Vail and surrounding Eagle County communities, holds water in the pool and is among those relying on the emergency framework. River district manager Andy Mueller said the priority in distributing available water is people and food production over other uses, including what he described as requests from wealthy recreational ranchers. Officials say the coordinated response has so far prevented the worst-case scenario of communities actually running out of drinking water.

joke.joke: My GPS told me to turn left, and I thought, 'I don't think that's right.' But the GPS was pretty confident, and I am not, so I turned left.

topStory.headline: U.S., Iran Hold Indirect Talks in Qatar on Shipping, Nuclear Issues; Both Sides Agree to Continue Negotiations
topStory.summary:
The United States and Iran concluded two days of indirect, mediator-led technical talks in Doha, Qatar on Wednesday, with Qatar's Foreign Ministry announcing that "positive progress" had been made on issues tied to the Islamabad Memorandum of Understanding. The discussions, conducted through Qatari and Pakistani intermediaries, focused primarily on commercial shipping conditions in the Strait of Hormuz and the unfreezing of Iranian assets. Both delegations agreed to continue negotiations, with the next round to be scheduled as soon as possible after funeral processions for Iran's former Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, which are planned from July 4 through July 9.

President Donald Trump expressed optimism from Washington, telling reporters that "the denuclearization of Iran is moving along well" and that the parties had held "very good meetings." Vice President JD Vance, speaking in Virginia Beach, said the talks were "going well" but cautioned that the nuclear issue was still ahead, noting that discussions on that front would start soon. Vance also declined to guarantee that the United States would not return to full combat operations, saying the president would not send the military back in "unless he has to."

The talks are grounded in a 14-point interim accord signed last month, intended to halt the war that began with U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran on February 28 and to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, while establishing a 60-day window for permanent peace negotiations. The two sides have publicly sparred over the meaning of the interim pact, and tit-for-tat military strikes occurred in the past week, underscoring the fragility of the ceasefire. Shipping through the strait remains far below pre-war levels, with roughly 35 commercial vessels transiting in the past 24 hours compared to an average of approximately 110 per day before the conflict.

Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi issued a separate warning Wednesday, threatening an "immediate powerful response" to any Israeli attack on Iran, after Israeli Defense Minister Israel Katz reportedly said Iranian Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei was "marked for death." Senior Iranian sources indicated that Tehran is determined to secure international recognition of its control over the Strait of Hormuz and its authority to levy transit fees on vessels, by force if necessary. Oil prices responded to the diplomatic activity by falling to their lowest level in four months, with WTI Crude futures dropping to approximately $67.89 per barrel and Brent Crude falling to $70.88.

The easing of geopolitical tensions contributed to a broader market rally, with the S&P 500 posting a gain of nearly 15% in the second quarter, its strongest quarterly performance since 2020. The next scheduled round of U.S.-Iran negotiations is set to take place after July 9, following the conclusion of the Khamenei funeral observances. The outcome of those talks is expected to determine whether the 60-day peace negotiation window produces a lasting agreement or risks a return to open conflict.
